A target market determination is a mandatory public document that sets out the class of consumers a financial product is likely to be appropriate for (target market) and matters relevant to the product’s distribution and review. An issuer and distributors of a financial product must take reasonable steps that will, or are reasonably likely to, result in distribution conduct relating to retail clients being consistent with the target market determination for the product.

entry.title: Former director of private lending companies permanently banned over fraud conviction
entry.summary: This article details the permanent ban issued by ASIC to a former director of two private lending companies due to a fraud conviction. Here are the key takeaways: * **Permanent Ban:** Andrew John Spira, former director of Skyecap Pty Ltd and Pineapple Funding Pty Ltd, is permanently banned by ASIC from providing financial services, engaging in credit activities, or controlling entities involved in those activities. * **Fraud Conviction:** The ban stems from Mr. Spira's conviction in a Northern Territory court for dishonestly obtaining a financial advantage by deception and other offenses including attempting to leave Australia on a false passport and using stolen credit card details. * **ASIC Authority:** ASIC has the legal authority under the Corporations Act and the National Consumer Credit Protection Act to permanently ban individuals convicted of fraud from participating in the financial services and credit industries. * **Effective Date:** The ban went into effect on November 19, 2025. * **Review Possible:** Mr. Spira has the right to appeal ASIC's decision to the Administrative Review Tribunal.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release discusses the permanent banning of a director of private lending companies (Skyecap and Pineapple Funding) from the financial services and credit industries due to a fraud conviction. This directly impacts the Banks and Thrifts & Mortgage Finance industries as these companies were involved in providing loans and credit services, and the regulatory action affects their operations and management. The reference to the National Consumer Credit Protection Act further solidifies this connection.
entry.risk_rationale: This release signals a moderate impact. The permanent ban on Andrew Spira from the financial services and credit industries due to fraud convictions necessitates changes for any entities associated with him. The text states he was a director of Skyecap and Pineapple Funding, both involved in SME lending, requiring them to replace him, potentially disrupting operations (background paragraphs). The reference to the Corporations Act and National Consumer Credit Protection Act suggests this action falls within existing regulatory frameworks, limiting the impact to those directly connected to Spira and similar actors. The ASIC's banned and disqualified persons register is also mentioned, suggesting an increased effort to monitor those in the financial services industry.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email Andrew John Spira, a former director of private credit lenders Skyecap Pty Ltd and Pineapple Funding Pty Ltd, has been permanently banned by ASIC from the financial services and credit industries after he was convicted of fraud by a Darwin court. Mr Spira was arrested in May 2023 after attempting to leave Australia on a false UK passport, attempting to pay for chartered flights using stolen credit card details, and making false declarations in an Australian passport application. On 17 November 2023, Mr Spira was convicted in the Northern Territory Local Court of numerous offences, including dishonestly obtaining a financial advantage by deception. He was sentenced to an 18-month good behaviour order and ordered to pay restitution. As a result of his fraud conviction, ASIC has permanently banned Mr Spira from: providing any financial services or engaging in any credit activities controlling, whether alone or in concert with one or more other entities, an entity that carries on a financial services business or another person who engages in credit activities, and performing any function involved in the carrying on of a financial services or engaging in credit activities. Under the Corporations Act and the National Consumer Credit Protection Act, ASIC may permanently ban a person from the financial services and credit industries if they are convicted of fraud. Mr Spira’s bannings took effect on 19 November 2025. Mr Spira has the right to apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal for a review of ASIC’s decision. Background Mr Spira was the Founder and CEO of Skyecap since October 2023 and was a director of Skyecap between February 2024 and July 2025. Skyecap is a private lender for small and medium businesses (SMEs) for loans ranging from $2,000 to $100,000. Mr Spira was the founder and CEO of Pineapple Funding between January 2019 and June 2024. He was also the sole director of Pineapple Funding between February 2019 and July 2025. Pineapple Funding was a loan broker based in Sydney that specialised in providing access to capital to SMEs. ASIC maintains a banned and disqualified persons register that provides information about people who have been disqualified from: involvement in the management of a corporation auditing self-managed superannuation funds (SMSFs), or practising in the financial services or credit industry. Moneysmart.gov.au has useful information for consumers about how to complain about a business .

entry.title: Cbus ordered to pay $23.5 million penalty for serious failures in processing members death benefits and insurance claims
entry.summary: Here's a summary of the article focusing on key information for compliance officers and business executives: Cbus, one of Australia's largest superannuation funds, has been penalized for significant failures in handling death benefits and total and permanent disability (TPD) insurance claims, resulting in substantial delays and distress for over 7,000 members. This outcome highlights the importance of robust claims processing systems, effective oversight of outsourced services, and timely reporting of breaches to regulators. * **Significant Penalty:** Cbus was ordered to pay a $23.5 million penalty for contravening the Corporations Act by failing to handle claims efficiently, honestly, and fairly. This penalty exceeds the company's declared revenue for the 2024 financial year. * **Systemic Failures and Outsourcing Issues:** The failures included unreasonable processing delays, inadequate data management, and insufficient oversight of its outsourced claims processing provider, Australian Administration Service Pty Limited (AAS). Cbus was aware AAS had not met its agreed service levels. * **Impact on Members:** The delays disproportionately affected vulnerable members and their families, exacerbating distress during difficult times. A significant percentage of death and TPD claims remained open for over 365 days. * **Remediation and Compliance:** In addition to the penalty, Cbus is required to undertake a remediation program to compensate affected members and implement a compliance program, including obtaining expert reports to ensure adequate systems are in place. They have already paid $32 million in compensation to affected members. * **Regulatory Scrutiny:** ASIC is increasing its focus on claims handling and member services failures within the superannuation industry, signaling a need for all funds to prioritize compliance and effective claims management.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release directly addresses failures in the handling of insurance claims by Cbus, a superannuation fund, impacting the **Insurance** industry group. The involvement of United Super Pty Ltd and the penalties imposed for breaches of the Corporations Act related to claims processing also directly affect the **Diversified Financials** industry group, which includes companies providing financial services like superannuation trustees.
entry.risk_rationale: This media release signifies a *High* regulatory impact due to the substantial $23.5 million penalty levied against Cbus for significant failures in handling insurance claims. This exceeds their declared revenue for FY24, indicating a major financial impact. The court found that Cbus contravened the Corporations Act by failing to handle claims efficiently, honestly and fairly, and failing to report the matters to ASIC within the required timeframe. ASIC's warning to the industry to "get it right" and increased scrutiny signals heightened compliance risk and the need for major operational changes across the superannuation sector. The case also highlights the significance of outsourcing arrangements and the responsibility of trustees to actively monitor service providers.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email One of Australia’s largest superannuation fund trustees has been ordered by the Federal Court to pay a penalty of $23.5 million for unreasonable delays experienced by thousands of members and claimants arising from serious failures to handle insurance claims in a timely manner. The penalty has been imposed against United Super Pty Ltd, the trustee of the Construction and Building Unions Superannuation Fund ( Cbus ), following admissions by Cbus that serious failures caused delays in processing death benefits and total and permanent disability (TPD) insurance claims and impacted more than 7,000 Australians in distressing situations. The penalty exceeds the $18.5m in revenue United Super declared in the 2024 financial year. The penalties come on top of Cbus’s own remediation program to pay approximately $32 million in compensation to the estimated 7,402 affected claimants and members for lost earnings and wrongfully charged fees. ASIC Deputy Chair Sarah Court said Cbus's failures needlessly exacerbated the distress of people who were already in upsetting situations. “Thousands of Australians suffered real and avoidable harm because of long delays and systemic failures in the way Cbus handled important and sensitive insurance claims,” said Ms Court. “When people were grieving the loss of a loved one or grappling with a life-altering injury, Cbus should have ensured timely and accurate decisions were made on their insurance claims.” “Not only was Cbus aware of increased insurance claim volumes, but it was also put on notice by its own customers who were complaining about the long delays they were enduring.” “This outcome underscores a message to the whole industry to get it right, especially when your members need it most. You cannot outsource your obligations to your members.” “ASIC has increased its scrutiny of claims handling and member services failures, which are both ASIC enforcement priorities, The sector should be on notice that ASIC is sharpening its focus,” the Deputy Chair said. In delivering his judgment, Justice O'Callaghan noted that, “Delays in the payment of benefits under insurance products can have serious and unacceptable consequences for affected members and claimants.” His Honour noted one case in which the widow of a Cbus member called into ABC radio in June 2023 to highlight a 15-month delay processing her late husband's death benefit claim. After she complained to the media about ongoing poor service, including a lack of communication and extensive periods on hold, Cbus commenced an investigation that led to the breach report to ASIC. His Honour said, “As at 30 June 2024, the Fund was ranked the ninth largest superannuation fund in Australia, and in the 2024 financial year the Fund had over $95 billion in total assets. As one of Australia's largest superannuation funds, it ought to have had more robust processes and systems in place to ensure compliance with key legislative obligations, and to prevent, promptly identify, and correct repeated individual and collective human errors resulting in failures to process claims within a reasonable timeframe.” Between October 2022 and November 2024, Cbus outsourced its claims processing to Australian Administration Service Pty Limited (AAS). However, His Honour further observed, “United Super was ultimately responsible for the outsourcing of material business activity and, from at least November 2022, various United Super executive and board committees were aware that AAS had not met its agreed service levels in relation to the processing of claims.” The Court found that between 27 March 2023 and 1 May 2023, as a percentage of all open claims, between 48% and 56% of all death claims (numbering between 438 and 479 claims) had been open for more than 365 days, and between 38% and 43% of all TPD claims (numbering between 391 and 409 claims) had been open for more than 365 days. The Court declared that Cbus contravened the Corporations Act by failing to do all things necessary to ensure Death, terminal illness and TPD claims ( Claims ) were handled efficiently, honestly and fairly by failing to: take all reasonable steps to ensure Claims were being processed in a reasonable period of time ensure that it held accurate and complete data necessary to determine the volume and age of all Claims ensure the relevant committees had sufficient oversight over issues with processing Claims, and adequately monitor and manage the administrator’s performance under the Administration Agreement. The Court also found Cbus had contravened the Corporations Act on two further occasions by failing to report the matters to ASIC within the required time frame. Reports were required to be made to ASIC on 3 March 2023 and 20 July 2023, but nothing was reported to ASIC until 5 August 2023. The report was made after criticism had occurred on an ABC Melbourne Radio program. In addition, Cbus was also ordered to pay half a million dollars towards ASIC’s legal costs and undertake a compliance program requiring it to obtain expert reports on whether it now has appropriate systems and processes in place. Download Judgment Background MUFG Pension & Market Services Holdings Limited, is a provider of services in Australia’s superannuation administration industry. It operates through its subsidiaries, including Australian Administration Services Pty Limited ACN 003 429 114 ( AAS ), which offers administrative services to United Super. Since 1 January 2021, United Super has been authorised under its Australian Financial Services licence to provide trustee services, including the handling of claims for Cbus Superannuation Fund. In 2021, United Super engaged AAS under an administration agreement to provide claims handling administration services with respect to Cbus. Those services included communicating with members, obtaining required documentary material and delivering this material to the Trustee and/or Insurer as the case may be. United Super self-reported the delays in processing claims to ASIC in August 2023. The above investigation was assisted by information received from the Australian Financial Complaints Authority ( AFCA ). AFCA is the external dispute resolution scheme for resolving complaints between consumers (including small businesses) and financial firms in Australia. AFCA is required under law to notify ASIC, APRA or the ATO about certain financial firm contraventions, breaches, failures to give effect to a determination and systemic issues that they become aware of when considering complaints, as appropriate. Further information is contained in Regulatory Guide 267 Oversight of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority ( RG 267 ) Complaints to AFCA about delays in claims handling by superannuation funds more than doubled between the 2022 and 2023 calendar years. Complaints about processing death benefit claims tripled over the same period. ASIC’s Moneysmart website features information that supports Australians in making decisions about insurance. Most super funds offer life, total permanent disability (TPD) and income protection insurance for their members. Find out more about how life insurance works and insurance through super .

entry.title: ASIC cancels AFS licence of Ivy League Capital
entry.summary: Here's a summary of the article, focusing on key information for compliance officers and business executives: The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) has cancelled the Australian Financial Services (AFS) license of Ivy League Capital Pty Ltd due to repeated non-compliance with regulatory requirements. This cancellation highlights the importance of adhering to financial reporting obligations and maintaining required memberships. Here are the key takeaways: * **AFS Licence Cancellation:** ASIC has cancelled Ivy League Capital's AFS licence (number 514905), effective November 17, 2025. * **Reason for Cancellation:** The cancellation stems from Ivy League Capital's failure to lodge audited financial reports for the year ended June 30, 2022, and subsequent years. * **AFCA Membership Lapse:** Ivy League Capital also failed to maintain membership with the Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) scheme. * **Authorised Services:** Ivy League Capital was authorised to deal in and provide financial product advice related to deposit and payment products, interests in managed investment schemes, and custodial or depository services for wholesale clients. * **Right to Appeal:** Ivy League Capital has the right to appeal ASIC’s decision to the Administrative Review Tribunal.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release discusses the cancellation of an AFS license for Ivy League Capital, which was authorized to deal in deposit and payment products, interests in managed investment schemes, and provide custodial or depository services. This directly impacts the Banks industry group due to the involvement with deposit and payment products. The Capital Markets industry group is affected because Ivy League Capital dealt with interests in managed investment schemes and provided custodial/depository services, indicating investment-related activities.
entry.risk_rationale: The cancellation of Ivy League Capital's AFS licence represents a Moderate impact. The firm failed to lodge audited financial reports and maintain AFCA membership, indicating a compliance failure with regulatory requirements. While the impact is limited to a single firm and wholesale clients, the cancellation itself requires the firm to cease its financial services business. The firm’s right to appeal introduces some uncertainty, but the effective date of November 2025 gives them time to comply or appeal.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email ASIC has cancelled the Australian financial services (AFS) licence of Ivy League Capital Pty Ltd (Ivy League Capital). This follows Ivy League Capital’s failure to lodge audited financial reports for the year ended 30 June 2022 and all subsequent years for which it was required to lodge audited financial reports. Ivy League also failed to maintain membership of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) scheme. The cancellation takes effect from 17 November 2025. Background Ivy League Capital Pty Ltd held AFS licence number 514905, granted on 5 September 2019. It was authorised to carry on a financial services business to deal in and provide financial product advice in relation to deposit and payment products, interests in managed investment schemes and provide custodial or depository services to wholesale clients. Ivy League has the right to appeal to the Administrative Review Tribunal for a review of ASIC’s decision.

entry.title: Sheffield Insurance directors convicted and fined over a five-year financial reporting failure
entry.summary: This article reports on the conviction and fines levied against two directors of Sheffield Insurance Pty Ltd for failing to lodge financial statements and auditor's reports with ASIC for five consecutive years. This case highlights ASIC's focus on financial reporting misconduct and the potential for directors to face individual criminal consequences for their company's reporting failures. Key takeaways for compliance officers and business executives: * **Directors Held Accountable:** Two directors were convicted and fined $15,000 each for failing to ensure their company, Sheffield Insurance, lodged required financial documents with ASIC. * **AFS Licensee Obligations:** Australian Financial Services (AFS) licensees are legally obligated to lodge financial statements and auditor's reports with ASIC within three months of the end of each financial year. * **ASIC Enforcement Priority:** ASIC is prioritizing the enforcement of financial reporting requirements and will take action against both companies and directors for reporting failures. * **Potential Penalties:** Failing to lodge financial statements and auditor’s reports to ASIC are criminal offences under 989B(2) and s989B(3) of the Corporations Act 2001 and can result in imprisonment and/or fines for both the company and its directors. * **Importance of Timely Reporting:** Timely financial reporting is crucial for ASIC to monitor the financial health of AFS licensees and ensure they meet their financial obligations.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release explicitly names "Sheffield Insurance Pty Ltd" and discusses the failure of its directors to lodge financial statements and auditor's reports. As such, the regulatory action directly targets and impacts companies operating within the Insurance industry.
entry.risk_rationale: The media release indicates a "Moderate" impact because it highlights ASIC's focus on financial reporting misconduct by AFS licensees. The conviction of directors and the statement that "Financial reporting misconduct is now an enforcement priority for ASIC" suggest increased scrutiny and potential for more enforcement actions. This requires AFS licensees to review their financial reporting processes and ensure compliance, potentially requiring "noticeable process/system changes" and "moderate resources." The specific mention of potential "individual criminal consequences" elevates the risk, moving it beyond a minor issue.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email Two directors of Sheffield Insurance Pty Ltd (Sheffield) have been convicted of two offences and fined $15,000 over their role in Sheffield failing to lodge financial statements and auditor’s reports with ASIC for five years. Phillip Bird of Alexander Heights, WA and Daniel Holmes of Beechboro, WA, were convicted and fined after each pleaded guilty to two charges of aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring Sheffield to commit an offence, one offence being the failure to lodge profit and loss statements and balance sheets (together, financial statements) and the second offence being the failure to lodge auditors’ reports. ASIC had alleged that, as Sheffield’s directors, Mr Bird and Mr Holmes failed to take steps to ensure that Sheffield lodged financial statements and auditors’ reports with ASIC for each financial year between 2019 and 2023 inclusive and were aware of the obligation to do so. As the holder of an Australian financial services (AFS) licence, Sheffield is required to lodge financial statements with ASIC within 3 months of the end of each financial year. ASIC Deputy Chair Sarah Court said, ‘It is important that AFS licensees lodge timely reports with ASIC so that ASIC can monitor their financial health and make sure they meet their financial obligations under law. Financial reporting misconduct is now an enforcement priority for ASIC and we will not hesitate to take action for reporting failures. Today’s decision show that directors can also face individual criminal consequences over their business’s reporting failures.’ The matter was prosecuted by the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (Cth) (CDPP). after a referral from ASIC. Mr Bird and Mr Holmes were also each ordered to pay court costs of $268.80. Background Australian financial services (AFS) licensees must have adequate financial resources to provide the financial services covered by their AFS licence and to carry out supervisory arrangements. FS Licensees are required to submit financial statements to ASIC to maintain investor confidence and ensure the accountability of management through the provision of timely and reliable financial information. The maximum available penalty for the offences is a term of 5 years imprisonment and/or fine of up to $126,000. However, when the charges are dealt with summarily (with the consent of prosecution and defence), the maximum penalty for each offence is 12 months’ imprisonment and/or a fine not exceeding 60 penalty units (at the relevant time, $12,600). Failing to lodge financial statements and auditor’s reports to ASIC are criminal offences under 989B(2) and s989B(3) of the Corporations Act 2001 .

entry.title: APRA releases response to minor framework updates for ADIs, insurers and RSE licensees
entry.summary: APRA has finalized minor updates to the prudential and reporting framework for ADIs, insurers, and RSE licensees. These updates, designed to keep the framework current between comprehensive reviews, follow a consultation period that began in September 2025. Here are the key takeaways: * **Minor Amendments Finalized:** APRA has finalized minor amendments to the prudential and reporting framework. * **Affected Entities:** The updates apply to Authorised Deposit-taking Institutions (ADIs), insurers, and Registrable Superannuation Entity (RSE) licensees. * **Purpose of Updates:** The changes aim to maintain the relevance and effectiveness of the prudential framework between major reviews. * **Documentation Available:** The response letter, finalized standards, and non-confidential submissions are available on the APRA website under "Minor updates to the prudential framework."
entry.industry_rationale: The media release explicitly mentions authorised deposit-taking institutions (ADIs), insurers, and registrable superannuation entity (RSE) licensees, which fall under the Banks, Insurance, and Financial Services Industry Groups respectively. APRA's role as the prudential regulator overseeing these entities and updating their prudential and reporting framework further solidifies the impact on these specific industry groups.
entry.risk_rationale: The media release explicitly states that the updates are "minor amendments" intended to "keep the prudential framework up-to-date between comprehensive reviews." This suggests a limited scope and impact. The release refers to "finalised prudential and reporting standards," indicating some level of procedural or reporting changes, but the "minor" descriptor implies that these changes are not substantial. It is assumed the consultation process refined the changes, and the impact should be within manageable parameters for the affected entities.
entry.full_text: Media Releases APRA releases response to minor framework updates for ADIs, insurers and RSE licensees Friday 21 November 2025 Print Email The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) has released a response to consultation on proposed minor amendments to the prudential and reporting framework for authorised deposit-taking institutions (ADIs), insurers and registrable superannuation entity (RSE) licensees. The response letter issued today follows the release of the proposed update for consultation in September 2025. These minor updates help APRA keep the prudential framework up-to-date between comprehensive reviews. The letter to industry, finalised prudential and reporting standards and non-confidential submissions are available on the APRA website at: Minor updates to the prudential framework . Media enquiries Contact APRA Media Unit, on +61 2 9210 3636 All other enquiries For more information contact APRA on 1300 558 849 . The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is the prudential regulator of the financial services industry. It oversees banks, mutuals, general insurance and reinsurance companies, life insurance, private health insurers, friendly societies, and most members of the superannuation industry. APRA currently supervises institutions holding around $9 trillion in assets for Australian depositors, policyholders and superannuation fund members.

entry.title: ASIC suspends AFSL of Centurion Capital Limited
entry.summary: Here's a summary of the article, focusing on key information for compliance officers and business executives: This article reports that the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) has suspended the Australian Financial Services (AFS) license of Centurion Capital Limited due to failures in meeting statutory audit and financial reporting obligations. * **AFS License Suspension:** ASIC has suspended Centurion Capital Limited's AFS license until April 21, 2026. * **Reason for Suspension:** The suspension is due to Centurion's failure to lodge required statutory audits and financial reports for both the company and the BTR Real Estate Investment Fund for the financial years ending 2022, 2023, and 2024. * **Potential Further Action:** ASIC may take further action at the end of the suspension period if Centurion does not rectify the breaches. * **Right to Appeal:** Centurion has the right to apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal for a review of ASIC's decision.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release directly names Centurion Capital Limited, an entity providing financial product advice and dealing in financial products, pointing to the Diversified Financials Industry Group. Furthermore, the company operates the BTR Real Estate Investment Fund, indicating a direct impact on the Real Estate Industry Group through the management and operation of real estate-related investments.
entry.risk_rationale: The suspension of Centurion Capital Limited's AFS licence until 2026 indicates a "High" impact. The text states that Centurion "failed to meet its statutory audit and financial reporting lodgement obligations" for multiple years, signifying serious compliance failures. This directly impacts Centurion's ability to "provide financial product advice and deal in a range of financial products, operate the Fund and provide custodial and depository services", causing major operational changes. The potential for further action by ASIC and the right for Centurion to appeal to the Administrative Review Tribunal further support a "High" impact rating.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email ASIC has suspended the Australian financial services (AFS) licence of Centurion Capital Limited (Centurion) until 21 April 2026. The AFS licence was suspended because Centurion failed to meet its statutory audit and financial reporting lodgement obligations for the company and the BTR Real Estate Investment Fund (Fund) for the financial years ending 2022, 2023 and 2024. At the end of the suspension period, ASIC may take further action if Centurion fails to rectify these breaches. Centurion may apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal for a review of ASIC’s decision. Background Centurion has held an AFSL since 22 December 2009. The licence authorises Centurion to provide financial product advice and deal in a range of financial products, operate the Fund and provide custodial and depository services to retail and wholesale clients.

entry.title: QLD shadow director charged with $8m debt factoring fraud involving Bunnings Warehouse
entry.summary: This article reports on charges filed against a Queensland man for alleged fraud and managing a corporation while disqualified, stemming from a debt factoring scheme involving falsified invoices purportedly issued to Bunnings Warehouse. Here are the key takeaways: * **Fraudulent Debt Factoring:** Brent Young, allegedly acting as a shadow director of HWT Group Australia Pty Ltd, is accused of creating over 10,000 fraudulent invoices to obtain over $8.3 million in advanced payments through a debt factoring agreement. * **Bunnings Warehouse Implication:** The fraudulent invoices were allegedly issued to Bunnings Warehouse, despite no business relationship between HWT Group and Bunnings for the goods described in the invoices. * **Disqualification:** At the time of the alleged offences (September 2020 - June 2023), Mr. Young was bankrupt and therefore disqualified from managing corporations. * **Legal Consequences:** Mr. Young faces charges under the Criminal Code 1899 (Qld) for fraud (maximum 20 years imprisonment) and the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) for managing a corporation while disqualified (maximum 5 years imprisonment). * **Court Adjournment:** The case has been adjourned to December 19, 2025, for mention.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release details a fraud involving debt factoring, a financial service provided by finance providers, and the fraudulent creation of invoices to obtain advanced payments. This directly impacts the Banks and Financial Services industries, as these entities are involved in providing and utilizing debt factoring services. The mention of a "Queensland-based finance provider" and the nature of debt factoring agreements underscore this connection.
entry.risk_rationale: The media release describes allegations of fraud and managing a corporation whilst disqualified, carrying maximum penalties of 20 and 5 years imprisonment, respectively. This suggests a *moderate* impact as it highlights potential compliance risks and could lead to increased scrutiny of debt factoring agreements and shadow director activities. The potential for significant prison sentences indicates a serious breach of corporate governance. While the case is still pending, the allegations involve a substantial sum ($8.3 million) and a well-known company (Bunnings), potentially prompting firms to review their internal controls related to financial transactions and director oversight.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email Mr Brent Young of Queensland has appeared at the Brisbane Magistrates Court today 21 November 2025 charged with one count of fraud and one count of managing a corporation whilst disqualified. Following an ASIC investigation, it is alleged that between 30 September 2020 and 30 June 2023 Mr Young, whilst acting as a shadow director of HWT Group Australia Pty Ltd (HWT Group), created and lodged over 10,000 fraudulent invoices with a Queensland-based finance provider to obtain over $8.3 million dollars in advanced payments under a debt factoring agreement Mr Young setup on behalf of HWT Group. Debt factoring is where a business makes an agreement with a third-party finance provider to sell its unpaid invoices (accounts receivable) at a discount to receive cash prior to the invoices being due for payment. ASIC alleges Mr Young created invoices issued to Bunnings Warehouse for payment of goods shipped to various stores, whilst neither Mr Young nor HWT Group had any existing business relationship with Bunnings relating to those goods. At the time of the offences, Mr Young was bankrupt and was therefore automatically disqualified from managing corporations. The matter has been adjourned to 19 December 2025 for mention. Background The charge of fraud is pursuant to section 408C of the Criminal Code 1899 (Qld) and carries a maximum penalty of 20 years’ imprisonment. The charge of managing a corporation whilst disqualified from doing so is pursuant to section 206A of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) and carries a maximum penalty of 5 years’ imprisonment. The matter is being prosecuted by the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (Cth) (CDPP).

entry.title: APRA publishes new report on financial system risks
entry.summary: APRA has released its inaugural System Risk Outlook report, a bi-annual publication designed to enhance transparency regarding domestic and international risks to inform regulatory priorities. The report highlights key vulnerabilities and provides insights from the first phase of APRA's system risk stress test. Here are the key takeaways for compliance officers and business executives: * **Heightened Geopolitical Risks:** The Australian financial system faces increased risks from overseas, with a volatile geopolitical environment expected to persist. APRA is addressing this through a dedicated geopolitical risk work program within the Council of Financial Regulators. * **Domestic Housing Vulnerabilities:** APRA is closely monitoring the Australian housing market, particularly high household debt and rising prices. While overall lending standards are sound, there are emerging signs of riskier lending practices, especially high debt-to-income borrowing by investors. * **Systemic Interconnectedness:** The increasing interconnectedness of the financial system raises the potential for shocks in one sector to propagate system-wide impacts. * **System Risk Stress Test Insights (Phase 1):** The initial phase of APRA's stress test indicates that superannuation funds can act as stabilizers during stress, but their actions can also amplify negative effects on members and the system in certain scenarios. Phase 2 of the stress test will further examine these findings.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release from APRA directly discusses risks and vulnerabilities within the Australian financial system. It specifically mentions the supervision of banks, insurance companies, and superannuation funds (which fall under Diversified Financials), and how APRA is monitoring risks related to housing lending standards and the interconnectedness of the financial system. The stress tests conducted with major banks and large superannuation funds further highlight the direct impact on these industry groups.
entry.risk_rationale: This APRA release signals *moderate* impact. The new *System Risk Outlook* report, published bi-annually, increases transparency and informs regulatory priorities, suggesting process changes for regulated entities. APRA's monitoring of housing market vulnerabilities and potential macroprudential interventions implies possible future constraints on lending practices. The inclusion of findings from the system risk stress test (Phase 1) and plans for Phase 2, impacting major banks and super funds, further indicates moderate resource allocation for compliance and potential system adjustments. This falls short of a fundamental shift, but necessitates noticeable adjustments within the financial system.
entry.full_text: Media Releases APRA publishes new report on financial system risks Thursday 20 November 2025 Print Email The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) has launched a new report on APRA’s assessment of risks and vulnerabilities facing the Australian financial system. The new System Risk Outlook report, which will be published twice a year, increases transparency around what APRA is seeing in the domestic and international risk environments to inform its regulatory priorities. Key insights from this first publication include: Risks to the Australian financial system from overseas are heightened, and the geopolitical environment is expected to remain volatile for some time. While the system is well-placed to absorb potential shocks from overseas, this resilience could be eroded if institutions are not prepared for a wide range of scenarios. APRA and the other agencies on the Council of Financial Regulators are strengthening the system’s resilience through a dedicated geopolitical risk work program. APRA is closely monitoring any build-up of domestic vulnerabilities, particularly in the housing market, including high household debt. While overall housing lending standards remain sound, APRA is seeing some signs of a pick-up in higher risk lending, particularly high debt-to-income borrowing by investors. The increasing interconnectedness of the financial system has elevated the potential for shocks in one sector to have a system-wide impact. Today’s report also includes a summary of the findings from Phase 1 of APRA’s inaugural system risk stress test, which was undertaken this year with the four major banks and six large superannuation funds. The key findings from Phase 1 suggest that the structural features of super funds mean that the industry acts as an important stabiliser for the system during stress but, in some cases, their actions can also amplify the negative effect of the shock on members and the system. APRA Chair John Lonsdale said: “The insights from our System Risk Outlook report confirm that Australia’s financial system is stable, resilient and well-placed to absorb shocks, but they also emphasise why we can’t be complacent. International political and economic uncertainty remains elevated, which is why APRA is stepping up its focus on geopolitical risk. “Domestically, housing remains a key vulnerability, given high household debt and prices continuing to rise. We are carefully monitoring these risks and ensuring banks are prepared to implement additional macroprudential tools where required to reinforce lending standards.” “Our inaugural system risk stress test is an important addition to APRA’s stress testing regime by helping us better understand how linkages in the financial system could dampen or amplify shocks. We will shortly commence Phase 2 of the exercise, which will test the robustness of the Phase 1 findings and consider other areas of analysis,” Mr Lonsdale said. APRA expects to publish a final detailed report of the findings of the system risk stress test, incorporating Phase 2, in mid-2026. The full System Risk Outlook report is available at: System Risk Outlook - November 2025 . Media enquiries Contact APRA Media Unit, on +61 2 9210 3636 All other enquiries For more information contact APRA on 1300 558 849 . The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is the prudential regulator of the financial services industry. It oversees banks, mutuals, general insurance and reinsurance companies, life insurance, private health insurers, friendly societies, and most members of the superannuation industry. APRA currently supervises institutions holding around $9 trillion in assets for Australian depositors, policyholders and superannuation fund members.

entry.title: Infrabuild companies pay infringement notices for failing to lodge financial reports on time
entry.summary: Here's a summary of the article, focusing on key takeaways for compliance officers and business executives: The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) issued infringement notices to two Infrabuild companies, part of the GFG Alliance, for failing to lodge their audited financial reports for the year ended June 30, 2024, within the statutory timeframe. The companies have paid the fines. This highlights the importance of timely and accurate financial reporting and underscores ASIC's increased focus on financial reporting misconduct. Key takeaways: * **Infringement Notices Issued:** Liberty Infrabuild Limited and Liberty Holdings Australia Pty Ltd each paid $187,800 in infringement notices for late lodgement of FY2024 financial reports. * **Late Lodgement & Audit Qualifications:** The financial reports were lodged significantly late (May and September 2025) and were issued with a qualified audit opinion and a disclaimer audit opinion, respectively. * **ASIC Enforcement Priority:** ASIC has identified failure to lodge financial reports as an enforcement priority in 2026, emphasizing the critical role of these reports for stakeholder decision-making and market transparency. * **Legal Obligation:** Companies have a strict legal obligation under s319(1) of the Corporations Act to lodge audited financial reports with ASIC, with penalties for contravention. * **Separate Civil Proceedings:** Separate civil proceedings were commenced in the Supreme Court of NSW in June 2025 against Whyalla Steelworks’ parent Liberty Primary Metals Australia Pty Ltd (now in voluntary administration), Tahmoor Coal Pty Ltd, and Liberty Bell Bay Pty Ltd for their failure to lodge various annual financial reports at the time the proceedings were commenced.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release directly involves Liberty Infrabuild Limited and Liberty Holdings Australia Pty Ltd, which are described as being involved in the manufacturing, distribution, and processing of steel long products and the recycling of scrap steel. Steel manufacturing and scrap metal recycling are core activities within the Materials sector, specifically under the Metals & Mining Industry Group. The failure to lodge financial reports on time directly affects the transparency and financial health assessment of these entities.
entry.risk_rationale: The infringement notices for late financial report lodgement, totaling $375,600, indicate a "Moderate" impact. The release highlights ASIC's increased enforcement priority for financial reporting misconduct in 2026, suggesting potential for more stringent oversight and penalties. The delayed lodgements, coupled with qualified and disclaimer audit opinions, raise concerns about market transparency and confidence. While the companies rectified the issue in FY25, the initial failure and ASIC's response necessitate process and system changes to ensure timely and accurate reporting, requiring moderate resources.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email Two Australian companies in the GFG Alliance group have paid infringement notices of $187,800 each, totalling $375,600, following their alleged failure to lodge audited financial reports for the year ended 30 June 2024 within the statutory timeframe. Liberty Infrabuild Limited and its parent company Liberty Holdings Australia Pty Ltd, own or operate businesses involved in the manufacturing, distribution and processing of steel long products and the recycling of scrap steel in Australia and globally. In the 2024 financial year, the group’s sales revenue was $5.2 billion and the group had liabilities of $2.19 billion (excluding liabilities from related parties). After failing to lodge by 31 October 2024, ASIC commenced inquiries with various GFG Alliance companies. Liberty Infrabuild subsequently lodged its financial report on 23 May 2025 with a qualified audit opinion, and Liberty Holdings Australia lodged on 15 September 2025 with a disclaimer audit opinion. At the ASIC Annual Forum last week, ASIC Deputy Chair Sarah Court announced that financial reporting misconduct, including failure to lodge financial reports, would be an enforcement priority for ASIC in 2026. ‘These reports provide critical information for investors, creditors and other stakeholders to make informed decisions. ‘Delays in lodgement undermine market transparency and confidence, particularly where audit reports are modified or subject to disclaimer opinions,’ the Deputy Chair said. Payment of an infringement notice does not constitute an admission of guilt or liability, and the companies are not regarded as having been convicted of the alleged offence. ASIC notes that both companies lodged their FY25 audited financial reports on time. Background GFG Alliance is a global group of businesses in industries including steel, aluminium, and energy. GFG Alliance has significant operations in Australia, including Infrabuild, Whyalla Steelworks in South Australia run by OneSteel Manufacturing Pty Limited (which is currently in voluntary administration), Tahmoor Coal in New South Wales, and Liberty Bell Bay in Tasmania. Separate civil proceedings were commenced in the Supreme Court of NSW in June 2025 against Whyalla Steelworks’ parent Liberty Primary Metals Australia Pty Ltd (now in voluntary administration), Tahmoor Coal Pty Ltd, and Liberty Bell Bay Pty Ltd for their failure to lodge various annual financial reports at the time the proceedings were commenced. The current status of these proceedings are outlined in ASIC’s media release: 25-141MR Three GFG Alliance Group companies ordered to lodge outstanding annual reports with ASIC . Infringement notices ASIC may issue an infringement notice if we believe, on reasonable grounds, that there has been a contravention of an infringement notice provision. A company has an obligation to lodge audited financial reports with ASIC under s319(1) of the Corporations Act and contravention is an offence of strict liability. Based on the applicable penalty units at the time of the alleged offence, the maximum penalty a court can impose is $375,600 for each offence. Strict liability offences in the Corporations Act are infringement notice provisions and are issued at half the maximum penalty that a court could impose for the contravention. More information The specific reasons for ASIC’s concerns are set out in the infringement notice on the Infringement Notices Register .

entry.title: ASIC suspends AFS licence of Surety Compliance Limited
entry.summary: Here's a summary of the article, highlighting key information for compliance officers and business executives: This article details the Australian Securities and Investments Commission's (ASIC) suspension of Surety Compliance Limited's AFS license due to failures in meeting financial requirements and maintaining professional indemnity insurance. The suspension impacts Surety's ability to provide financial services, but allows for the winding up of the Private Investment Fund (Scheme). Key Takeaways: * **AFS Licence Suspension:** ASIC has suspended Surety Compliance Limited's AFS licence (number 322 620) until October 27, 2026. * **Reason for Suspension:** The suspension is due to Surety's failure to meet minimum financial requirements and hold professional indemnity insurance. * **Limited Permitted Activities:** Surety can continue to provide financial services related to the payment of redemptions and winding up of the Private Investment Fund (Scheme). * **Ongoing Obligations:** Surety is still required to maintain a dispute resolution system, be a member of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority, and hold professional indemnity insurance during the suspension. * **Right to Review:** Surety has the right to apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal for a review of ASIC's decision.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release directly concerns the suspension of an Australian Financial Services (AFS) license held by Surety Compliance Limited, a responsible entity of a Private Investment Fund. This directly impacts the Capital Markets industry group as AFS licenses are crucial for entities providing financial services within this sector, and the failure to meet financial requirements and maintain professional indemnity insurance affects the integrity and operation of investment funds. The suspension and associated requirements (dispute resolution, complaints authority membership) highlight regulatory oversight within capital markets.
entry.risk_rationale: The suspension of Surety Compliance Limited's AFS license until 2026 indicates a "Moderate" impact. While Surety can continue services related to redemptions and winding up the Scheme, the suspension itself (due to failure to meet financial requirements and hold professional indemnity insurance) necessitates process and system changes to rectify these deficiencies. The requirement to maintain dispute resolution and AFCA membership during suspension adds further compliance burden. Surety's right to appeal to the Administrative Review Tribunal introduces uncertainty, but the core issue of license suspension suggests noticeable resource allocation to address the underlying problems.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email ASIC has suspended the Australian financial services (AFS) licence of Surety Compliance Limited ( Surety ) until 27 October 2026. Surety is the responsible entity of the Private Investment Fund ( Scheme ). This follows Surety’s failure to meet minimum financial requirements and hold professional indemnity insurance. The terms of the suspension mean Surety may continue to provide financial services that are necessary for, or incidental to, the payment of redemptions from and winding up of the Scheme. During the suspension Surety is required to meet obligations, if applicable, to have a dispute resolution system and be a member of the Australian Financial Complaints Authority scheme and hold professional indemnity insurance cover. Surety may apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal for a review of ASIC's decision. Background Surety has held AFS licence number 322 620 since 24 October 2008.

entry.title: Australians report nearly $260M in losses as shopping scams surge
entry.summary: This article from the National Anti-Scam Centre highlights a significant increase in scam-related losses reported by Australians in the first nine months of 2025, with online shopping scams being a major contributor. Businesses and compliance officers should be aware of the evolving tactics used by scammers, particularly those leveraging social media and exploiting vulnerable communities. Here are the key takeaways: * **Significant Financial Losses:** Australians lost nearly $260 million to scams between January and September 2025, a 16% increase compared to the same period in 2024. * **Online Shopping Scams Surge:** Shopping scams are the most reported scam type involving financial loss, with losses increasing by 19% compared to 2024. Scammers exploit events like Black Friday sales. * **Social Media Account Compromises:** Scammers are increasingly compromising social media accounts (Facebook, Instagram) to target victims' networks, promoting fake sales, grants, and investment schemes. * **Vulnerable Communities Targeted:** There is a rise in reported financial losses among vulnerable communities, including people with disabilities, those who speak English as a second language, and First Nations people. * **Key Advice: Stop, Check, Protect:** The National Anti-Scam Centre urges individuals to take precautions: **Stop** before acting, **Check** the legitimacy of offers and websites, and **Protect** personal and financial information.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release discusses a surge in online shopping scams, directly impacting the Retailing industry as consumers are defrauded while attempting to make purchases. The scams often involve fake websites, advertisements, social media, and mobile apps, which implicates the Software & Services industry, as these platforms are exploited to facilitate the scams.
entry.risk_rationale: This ACCC release highlights a significant increase in scam losses, particularly online shopping scams (+19% in losses). While not introducing new regulations directly, the figures ($260M in losses) and the ACCC's call for increased vigilance suggest a moderate impact. Businesses, especially those with online presence, will need to enhance their security measures and consumer awareness efforts to mitigate the risk of their platforms being used for scams and to protect their customers. The increased targeting of vulnerable communities also necessitates targeted outreach and support.
entry.full_text: Date 18 November 2025 Topics Scams Australians reported nearly $260 million in losses to scams in the first nine months of 2025, with the National Anti-Scam Centre warning that online shopping scams are on the rise. Between January and September 2025, the National Anti-Scam Centre’s Scamwatch service received 159,319 scam reports with financial losses of $259.5 million. This represents a 16 per cent increase in losses and a 20 per cent decrease in reports compared to the same period last year. As Australians engage with Black Friday sales, shoppers are urged to stay alert for scams disguised as genuine deals. Shopping scams were the most reported scam type involving financial loss so far this year, with 9,628 of the total 19,662 reports received resulting in $8.6 million in losses. This was an increase of 19 per cent in reported losses from the same period in 2024. “Scammers love Black Friday sales too because they know shoppers are looking for bargains and they rely on creating urgency and pressure that can come with a busy shopping period,” ACCC Deputy Chair Catriona Lowe said. “We remind consumers to take their time, check the legitimacy of websites and its offers, and be cautious about sharing personal or financial information online. A few simple checks before making a purchase can make all the difference in avoiding a scam and keeping your money safe.” Online content, such as fake websites, advertisements, social media, and mobile apps, were the most common method used by scammers for initial contact, resulting in $122 million in losses, or 47 per cent of overall scam losses. Some shopping scams involve the compromise of social media accounts to reach unsuspecting victims. Compromised social media accounts are increasingly used by scammers to target victims’ personal networks, particularly on Facebook and Instagram. After gaining access, scammers impersonate the account holder to promote scams such as fake ticket sales, ‘fire sales’, grant offers, and investment schemes. They further compromise friends’ accounts by requesting one-time codes under false pretences, enabling a chain of account takeovers. Example of a social media post from a compromised account, advertising a ‘fire sale’ of items. "We continue to urge Australians to verify who they are communicating with online, as scammers leverage trusted relationships and social platforms to manipulate people into handing over money and personal information,” Ms Lowe said. “You should be aware that online friend accounts can be compromised, so avoid clicking on any links and always do your own checks before paying money. A good way to check is to contact them through another means, such as by phone or text message. This can also alert your friend so they can take prompt action to recover their account.” The National Anti-Scam Centre is also seeing more people report financial loss among vulnerable communities; with a 12 per cent rise for people with a disability, 35 per cent for those who speak English as a second language, and 50 percent for First Nations people. Scamwatch website recently published resources for people to provide support to loved ones who may be targeted by scammers, including red flag signs , conversation starters and the manipulation tactics deployed by scammers. “We strongly encourage people to check in with their loved ones and start the conversation about scams. Scammers are criminals who target anyone,” Ms Lowe said. “Starting an honest conversation about scams can take away the fear and embarrassment that often stop people from speaking up. Listening with compassion and without judgement helps them feel supported and understood.” “We’re grateful to everyone who reports scams to Scamwatch, even if they haven’t lost money. Each report helps us build a clearer picture of scam activity and strengthens our work with other organisations to disrupt these criminal networks.” Other scam trends Investment scams were the cause of the highest overall reported losses in the first nine months of 2025, totalling $128.4 million. However, investment scams was one of the few scam categories where the losses showed a decrease from the same period in 2024 (down from $135 million). There was a 50 per cent decrease in reports about rebate scams, dropping from 3,236 reports in 2024 to 1,628 reports in 2025. There were 11,602 reports of false billing scams in 2025 compared to 24,139 in 2024, which was a 52 per cent decrease. In 2025, there were $19.4 million in reported losses to false billing scams compared to $11.3 million in 2024. There was also a 47 per cent drop in reports about remote access scams from 2024 to 2025. Losses to these scams also decreased by 30 per cent; down from $5.9 million in 2024 to $4.2 million in the same period in 2025. Remote access scams disproportionately impact older Australians. There were 52,753 reports of phishing scams made to Scamwatch in 2025, down from 80,119 in 2024. This is a decrease of 34 percent. Learn how to Stop. Check. Protect. to stay safe from scams: STOP. Always take a moment before giving your money or personal information to anyone. Scammers will create a sense of urgency to pressure you into acting quickly. Don’t rush to make decisions about money or sharing personal details. What to do: Say no, hang up, or delete suspicious messages Take time to think before responding to unexpected requests Don’t let anyone pressure you into immediate action Trust your instincts if something feels wrong CHECK. Make sure the person or organisation you’re dealing with is real. Scammers pretend to be from organisations you know and trust. Always verify who you’re really dealing with before taking any action. What to do: Contact the person or organisation directly using phone numbers or email addresses you find on their official website or app Research investment opportunities or offers through official sources like ASIC Get a second opinion from family, friends, or professionals PROTECT. Act quickly it something feels wrong. The sooner you act, the better you can protect yourself and others from scammers. What to do: Contact your bank immediately if you think you’ve lost money or shared financial details Contact IDCARE ( www.idcare.org or call 1800 595 160) if you want support to recover - they can help you create a plan to the limit damage of scams Report to Scamwatch ( www.scamwatch.gov.au ) to help protect others Report to police ( www.cyber.gov.au ) Change passwords and security details if you think they’ve been compromised Monitor your bank statements and credit reports for unusual activity Report the scam to the impersonated organisation and platform where the scam is happening Being scammed can feel overwhelming. Support is available at Lifeline on 13 11 14 or Beyond Blue on 1300 22 4636 Release number 130/25 General enquiries Submit an online enquiry to ask a question or you can make a report to help us understand where there are problems. Media enquiries media@accc.gov.au or 1300 138 917. The media team is only able to answer requests and questions from journalists. Please only call this number if you are a journalist.

entry.title: Farmers and families urged to stay safe on quad bikes
entry.summary: The ACCC has launched a safety awareness campaign focusing on quad bike safety in regional Australia due to the high number of injuries and fatalities, particularly among older male farmers and children. Compliance officers and business executives should be aware of the following key points: * **High-Risk Groups:** Older male farmers (over 70) are the most at-risk group for fatal quad bike incidents, with children also facing significant risks. * **Key Safety Measures:** The ACCC emphasizes wearing helmets, fitting rollover protection (Operator Protection Devices), and preventing children from riding adult quad bikes. * **Safety Standard Compliance:** The Consumer Goods (Quad Bikes) Safety Standard mandates minimum stability levels, rollover protection, and safety warnings for new and imported second-hand quad bikes. Businesses should ensure compliance with these standards. * **Older Quad Bikes:** A significant number of older quad bikes without rollover protection are still in use, posing a safety risk. The ACCC urges investment in rollover protection for these vehicles. * **Awareness Campaign:** The ACCC is running an awareness campaign across regional Australia, reminding users that quad bikes are powerful machines and not toys.
entry.industry_rationale: The ACCC's quad bike safety awareness campaign directly impacts the Consumer Discretionary sector as it involves the sale and use of recreational vehicles. It also impacts the Industrials sector because the manufacturing and safety standards of quad bikes fall under industrial machinery and equipment. The Materials sector is affected because the manufacturing of the quad bikes and their safety features relies on raw materials.
entry.risk_rationale: This ACCC release focuses on raising awareness about quad bike safety. While the Consumer Goods (Quad Bikes) Safety Standard is mentioned, the release primarily promotes adherence to existing safety guidelines and encourages the adoption of rollover protection on older quad bikes ("We urge anyone with an older quad bike to invest in rollover protection"). The campaign ("Quad bike safety awareness campaign") targets behavioural changes rather than imposing new mandatory requirements. Therefore, the regulatory impact is low, involving limited cost and scope related to voluntary safety upgrades.
entry.full_text: Date 14 November 2025 Topics Buying and selling products and services Problem with a product or service Stay protected The ACCC has launched a quad bike safety awareness campaign in regional Australia warning that older male farmers remain the group most at risk of fatal injuries or crashes, with children also at risk. Since 2011, 234 Australians have died in quad bike incidents, with men accounting for more than 80 per cent of fatalities, figures from SafeWork Australia show. “Quad bikes pose a serious risk of injury and death because it is easy for them roll over, even at low speeds. When they rollover, riders are often trapped underneath them. These heavy vehicles cause suffocation, head injuries and crush body parts,” ACCC Deputy Chair Mick Keogh said. “Most quad bike deaths occur on farms, and men aged over 70 years are statistically the most at-risk age group. More than 20 per cent of fatalities have been males over the age of 70.” Children under 14 make up more than one in ten deaths, while children under 16 make up more than a quarter of all emergency department presentations relating to quad bike injuries. The ACCC’s newly launched safety awareness campaign is warning farmers, partners, parents and carers of the dangers of quad bikes. The ads warn that One ride can change everything and encourages partners, parents and carers to Protect your loved ones. They will appear on online news sites throughout regional Australia, as well as social media platforms, Spotify and on various podcasts. “We want to remind all quad bike users, including parents of children who may be spending time on a farm this summer holiday period, that quad bikes are not toys, they are powerful machines,” Mr Keogh said. “There are three golden rules to follow when riding quad bikes – helmet on, rollover protection fitted and never let children ride adult quad bikes. Use these safety tips to protect your loved ones and family members." NSW and Queensland have the highest number of quad bike related injuries and fatalities. Rollovers and collisions are the most likely cause of death from quad bikes across all states, SafeWork Australia figures show. The Consumer Goods (Quad Bikes) Safety Standard was introduced to reduce the risk of injury or fatality caused by quad bikes. It requires new and imported second hand quad bikes to have minimum stability levels, include rollover protection, and display clear safety warnings at the point of sale. “The Safety Standard has resulted in many safer quad bikes being used, but there is likely to still be hundreds of thousands of older bikes without rollover protection in use,” Mr Keogh said. “We urge anyone with an older quad bike to invest in rollover protection. The cost is minimal compared to the cost of serious injury or a fatality.” “The transition to safer quad bike use will take time and continued awareness.” ACCC’s quad bike safety awareness campaign Quad bike safety: Wear a helmet. Use a quad bike with rollover protection/an operator protection device. You can have one fitted on older quad bikes. Never let children ride adult quad bikes, even as passengers. Don’t carry any passengers on quad bikes that are meant for one person. Avoid riding on rough terrain or steep slopes. Prepare safely before every ride: Always tell someone where you are planning to go and when you expect to return. Make sure you are properly trained before you start using your quad bike Wear protective clothing and gear. Besides a helmet, this may include goggles, long sleeves, long pants, boots and gloves. Never ride under the influence of alcohol or drugs. Always carry a mobile phone or radio device so you can get help in case of an emergency. Release number 129/25 General enquiries Submit an online enquiry to ask a question or you can make a report to help us understand where there are problems. Media enquiries media@accc.gov.au or 1300 138 917. The media team is only able to answer requests and questions from journalists. Please only call this number if you are a journalist.

entry.title: APRA releases notes for ICA CEO roundtable on striking the right regulatory balance - October 2025
entry.summary: Here's a summary of the article, focusing on key takeaways for compliance officers and business executives: This article announces the release of notes from an Insurance Council of Australia (ICA) CEO roundtable held in October 2025, where APRA and ASIC engaged with general insurance industry executives to discuss achieving the right regulatory balance. Key takeaways: * **APRA Roundtable:** APRA Chair John Lonsdale hosted a roundtable with ICA CEOs to discuss regulatory balance in the general insurance industry. * **Stakeholder Involvement:** The roundtable included participation from APRA, ASIC, and executives from the general insurance industry. * **Publicly Available Notes:** The notes from the roundtable are available on the APRA website. * **APRA's Role:** APRA is the prudential regulator overseeing various financial institutions, including general insurance companies, holding approximately $9 trillion in assets.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release explicitly mentions the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA) CEO roundtable and focuses on striking the right regulatory balance within the general insurance industry. APRA's involvement as the prudential regulator overseeing general insurance and reinsurance companies further solidifies the impact on the Insurance industry group.
entry.risk_rationale: The media release announces the availability of notes from a roundtable discussion between APRA, ASIC, and ICA CEOs. The topic was "striking the right regulatory balance," suggesting a review or potential adjustments to existing regulations rather than entirely new mandates. While the discussion involved senior figures, the release itself is informational. The impact is likely low, pertaining to minor procedural or reporting updates that may arise from the discussed balance, but without seeing the notes themselves, the full scope is uncertain.
entry.full_text: Media Releases APRA releases notes for ICA CEO roundtable on striking the right regulatory balance - October 2025 Thursday 13 November 2025 Print Email APRA Chair John Lonsdale delivered opening remarks at the Insurance Council of Australia (ICA) CEO roundtable on striking the right regulatory balance which was held on 30 October 2025. Mr Lonsdale welcomed views from executives from the general insurance industry and was joined by APRA colleagues and senior representatives from the Australian Securities and Investment Commission (ASIC). The notes can be found on the APRA website at: Insurance Council of Australia roundtable on striking the right regulatory balance - October 2025 . Media enquiries Contact APRA Media Unit, on +61 2 9210 3636 All other enquiries For more information contact APRA on 1300 558 849 . The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) is the prudential regulator of the financial services industry. It oversees banks, mutuals, general insurance and reinsurance companies, life insurance, private health insurers, friendly societies, and most members of the superannuation industry. APRA currently supervises institutions holding around $9 trillion in assets for Australian depositors, policyholders and superannuation fund members.

entry.title: ASIC sues Interprac over alleged Shield and First Guardian licensee failures
entry.summary: ASIC has initiated legal action against Interprac Financial Planning, alleging failures in oversight and compliance that led to significant risks and poor advice for thousands of clients who invested in the now-collapsed Shield Master Fund and First Guardian Master Fund. The suit centers on Interprac's alleged failures in ensuring its representatives acted in clients' best interests and in maintaining adequate risk management systems. Here are the key allegations and implications: * **Breach of Best Interests Duty:** ASIC alleges Interprac failed to ensure its representatives complied with their best interests obligations when advising clients to invest in Shield and First Guardian, leading to approximately $677 million of superannuation being invested in these funds. * **Inadequate Product Approval Process:** Interprac allegedly lacked a proper process for approving financial products, including Shield and First Guardian, for its approved product list, relying solely on external research. * **Failure to Respond to Red Flags:** ASIC claims Interprac failed to adequately respond to warning signs, including the use of lead generators, payments to related parties, and internal concerns about the funds. * **Compliance Failures:** Interprac is accused of failing to enforce investment holds, prevent "negative consent" practices, address client complaints appropriately, and take meaningful action in response to repeated compliance issues identified in audits. * **Potential Penalties:** ASIC is seeking declarations, civil penalties, and orders to restrain Interprac from carrying on a financial services business.
entry.industry_rationale: The media release discusses ASIC's legal action against Interprac for failures related to financial advice and risk management concerning superannuation investments. This directly impacts the Banks industry as superannuation funds often utilize banking services for asset management and investment activities. The Capital Markets industry is affected because the case involves financial advisors and investment products (Shield and First Guardian Master Funds), highlighting regulatory concerns about the integrity of capital market operations and investment advice.
entry.risk_rationale: This release indicates a High impact due to the serious allegations against Interprac, including failing to ensure its representatives acted in clients' best interests, resulting in approximately $677 million of superannuation being invested in unsuitable, high-risk funds that have since collapsed ("thousands of Australians were exposed to poor financial advice and significant risks"). ASIC is seeking declarations, civil penalties, and orders to restrain Interprac from carrying on a financial services business. The allegations of systemic failures in product approval, risk management, and complaint handling suggest a need for major operational and financial changes within Interprac and potentially other licensees. This also raises significant compliance risk for Interprac and highlights strategic concerns about licensee oversight responsibilities.
entry.full_text: Print Share Twitter Facebook LinkedIn Email Thousands of Australians were exposed to poor financial advice and significant risks from the Shield Master Fund (Shield) and First Guardian Master Fund (First Guardian) through critical oversight and compliance failures by Interprac Financial Planning Pty Ltd (Interprac), ASIC alleges in new proceedings filed today with the Federal Court. ASIC has commenced civil penalty proceedings in the Federal Court against Interprac for allegedly failing to ensure its former authorised representatives Venture Egg (a corporate partnership), and Rhys Reilly Pty Ltd (together, Representatives), complied with the best interests obligations and for failing to have adequate risk management systems. Together, these Representatives advised around 6,843 clients to invest around $677 million of their superannuation into Shield and First Guardian. Both funds have now collapsed, leaving people’s superannuation at risk. ASIC alleges Interprac failed to: have in place an adequate process for approving financial products it allowed onto its approved product list, including Shield and First Guardian, and relied entirely on external research to add those funds to its approved investments list for advisers; respond appropriately to the use of lead generators (being Imperial Capital Group Australia Pty Ltd and AGAT Business Pty Ltd (in liquidation)); respond adequately to news that payments had been made to Mr Ferras Merhi’s companies by entities associated with First Guardian and Shield; enforce or maintain a hold on new investments into Shield and First Guardian after Interprac’s Managing Director and Responsible Manager, Garry Crole, acknowledged serious issues with both funds; prevent the use of a ‘negative consent’ practice, which led to some clients’ superannuation being invested in Shield and/or First Guardian without express consent from those clients; respond adequately to significant inflows of investment into Shield and First Guardian; provide adequate responses to client complaints about advice from the Representatives to invest in Shield or First Guardian and instead relied on a ‘template’ response which often failed to consider the appropriateness of the advice; and respond adequately or impose meaningful consequences in response to serious compliance issues, including failings repeatedly identified in audits. ASIC Deputy Chair Sarah Court said ‘Interprac’s alleged oversight and compliance failures exposed thousands of Australians to poor advice and significant financial risk. ‘We allege Interprac failed to ensure certain authorised representatives acted in their clients’ best interests, contributing to hundreds of millions of dollars of superannuation being invested in products that were unsuitable, high risk and costly. ‘We allege that no competent financial adviser could have recommended Australians invest large amounts of their superannuation in these funds, and that Interprac – as licensee – should have been alert and responsive to the significant risk this conduct posed to clients, but it failed on many levels,’ the Deputy Chair said. ASIC is seeking declarations, civil penalties, and orders to restrain Interprac from carrying on a financial services business. Background Effective 31 May 2025, Mr Ferras Merhi and Venture Egg are no longer authorised representatives of Interprac. Effective 15 August 2025, Mr Rhys Reilly and Rhys Reilly Pty Ltd are no longer authorised representatives of Interprac. ASIC has commenced separate proceedings against Ferras Merhi over Shield and First Guardian ( 25-184MR ). ASIC has secured Federal Court interim orders restraining Ferras Merhi from operating within the financial services industry ( 25-249MR ). Downloads Originating Process (PDF 522 KB) Concise Statement (PDF 564 KB) Consumer information ASIC has issued a consumer alert warning amid increasing concerns that people are being enticed to invest their retirement savings into complex and risky schemes ( 25-120MR Consumer alert ). Be super smart, visit ASIC’s Moneysmart campaign page . Stay Updated: ASIC will post important updates on its dedicated webpages: Shield Master Fund and the First Guardian Master Fund .
